mnemonics.title: SMEM-GMEM Speed Contrast
mnemonics.content: SMEM (Shared Memory) is like a hot desk right next to you - super fast but tiny space. GMEM (Global Memory) is like the library basement - huge but takes time to get there.

mnemonics.title: CUDA Thread Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: Think of it like a company: Grid = Company (all workers), Blocks = Departments (groups), Threads = Individual workers. Each knows their department (block) and employee ID (thread).

mnemonics.title: Warp Size记忆
mnemonics.content: WARP = 32 threads. Remember: WARP sounds like 'twarp' - 32 letters? No! Just remember: 32 bits in a byte, 32 threads in a warp!

acronyms.usage: NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and API for GPU programming

acronyms.expansion: General-Purpose computing on Graphics Processing Units
acronyms.usage: Using GPUs for non-graphics parallel computations
The core processing unit of a GPU containing multiple CUDA cores
GPU execution model where threads execute the same instruction but on different data
Programming paradigm where same program runs on different data points in parallel

visualTips.visualization: Imagine 32 people doing jumping jacks in perfect sync - that's a warp. If one person does pushups instead, there's divergence - the warp slows down!
Think of grocery checkout: 32 people with consecutive items (coalesced) = one fast transaction. 32 people with items scattered everywhere (non-coalesced) = 32 slow transactions.
CPU = 4-16 fancy chefs in one kitchen (sequential, complex tasks). GPU = 1000s of fast food workers (simple, parallel tasks). Both needed for different jobs!

analogies.analogy: A GPU is like a massive factory floor. The host (CPU) is the manager who decides what to make. Each SM is a work station with many workers (threads). They all work on similar tasks simultaneously - like assembling different parts of the same product.

Shared memory is like a whiteboard in a meeting room. Everyone in the team (block) can quickly read/write to it, but only during the meeting (block execution). Once the meeting ends (block completes), the whiteboard is cleared.

Calling a kernel with <<<grid, block>>> is like sending out a team of delivery drivers. Grid tells you how many teams, block tells you how many drivers per team. Each driver (thread) knows their route (thread ID) and delivers to one house (element).
